title: Log-Noetherian Functions
speaker: Gal Binyamini
abstract:
I'll talk about the o-minimal structures R_LN and R_{LN,exp} where one has an effective form of the finiteness property of o-minimality. Unlike the more classical structure of Pfaffian function, R_{LN,exp} contains the period integrals for aribtrary algebraic families. The existence of such a structure was conjectured (in slightly weaker form) by Khovanskii in the eighties.
I'll explain the definition of the structure and illustrate the main ideas from hyperbolic geometry, commutative algebra and differential equations that lead to effective bounds.

proof_stages.label: Classical Noetherian chains and Khovanskii's effective finiteness problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with holomorphic Noetherian chains on a polydisk, their format/complexity data, and Khovanskii's conjecture that isolated solutions of Noetherian equations admit effective bounds.

proof_stages.label: Log-Noetherian category and effective o-minimal theorem
proof_stages.summary: Ordinary derivatives are replaced on punctured disks and annuli by logarithmic derivations x_j d/dx_j, leading to Log-Noetherian functions and the theorem that the resulting structures R_LN and R_LN,exp are effective o-minimal structures.

proof_stages.label: Cellular parameterization theorem
proof_stages.summary: For finitely many Log-Noetherian functions on a slightly extended cell, one constructs an effective finite cellular cover on which each pullback is identically zero or nowhere vanishing.

proof_stages.label: CPT as effective local resolution and monomialization
proof_stages.summary: The cellular parameterization is interpreted as a resolution process: after passing to charts, nonvanishing functions become constants times monomials times effectively bounded Log-Noetherian units, paralleling principalization of ideals.

proof_stages.label: Hyperbolic bounded-ratio lemma
proof_stages.summary: A key analytic substitute for compactness says that a holomorphic map from an extended cell to C\{0,1} is uniformly bounded on the smaller cell, proved by hyperbolic contraction for disks and by a universal-cover argument for annuli.

proof_stages.label: Necessity of logarithmic closure under units
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains why ordinary Noetherian functions are not stable under the unit factors created by monomialization, whereas the Log-Noetherian class is designed to be stable in the induction.

proof_stages.label: Variation of argument from effective linearization
proof_stages.summary: Quantitative bounds come from controlling variation of argument: derivative ideals in the Noetherian ring stabilize effectively, producing a linear differential equation with bounded coefficients to which de la Vallée Poussin-type zero/argument estimates apply.

proof_stages.label: Induction to several variables via resolved hypersurfaces
proof_stages.summary: The one-dimensional variation bounds are lifted to systems in several variables by desingularizing one equation, restricting the remaining equations to the resulting curves, and iterating the argument.

examples.name: High-frequency sine/cosine Noetherian chain
examples.note: The chain generated by sin(mx) and cos(mx) is used as a counterexample to any format that ignores coefficient size, since it has many zeros while remaining bounded.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker checks that the high-frequency sine/cosine functions form a Noetherian chain and computes their zero growth as about m/2π to motivate coefficient dependence

passes.reason: the high-frequency sine/cosine chain is used to show that zero-count bounds cannot ignore coefficient-size data since zeros grow with m
Used as an ordinary Noetherian chain with arbitrarily many zeros to refute bounds that ignore coefficient size.
The chain generated by sin(mx) and cos(mx) is a standard oscillatory Noetherian family used to refute bounds that ignore the coefficient/frequency m.
An ordinary high-frequency trigonometric Noetherian chain is used to show zero counts grow with the frequency parameter, so coefficient-free effective bounds cannot hold.
The chain generated by sin(mx), cos(mx) is an ordinary Noetherian chain used to show bounds cannot ignore coefficient/frequency data.
used to show coefficient-independent Noetherian bounds fail because high-frequency sin(mx), cos(mx) have many zeros
used to show coefficient-free Noetherian bounds fail, while the high-frequency trigonometric chain is not intrinsically extremal, degenerate, maximally symmetric, or sporadic
The chain generated by sin(mx) and cos(mx) is used to show that bounds cannot ignore coefficient/format size, but it is just an ordinary parametric Noetherian chain.
Used to show coefficient-independent effective bounds fail, and the sine/cosine chain is the highly regular rotational oscillator.
used to show coefficient-independent effective bounds fail, while the trigonometric chain is a highly regular periodic linear system
the highly regular periodic sin(mx),cos(mx) chain is used to show coefficient-size data cannot be omitted from effective bounds
The high-frequency sin(mx), cos(mx) chain is used to show coefficient-free Noetherian bounds cannot hold, but it is just a standard oscillatory family.

examples.name: Period integrals and period maps
examples.note: Period integrals, viewed through Gauss-Manin regular singular connections, illustrate the intended geometric applications and why singular/logarithmic behavior matters.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker writes the local expansion of period integrals and traces why the factors are log-Noetherian or exponential-definable, concluding period maps lie in the structure

passes.reason: period integrals and period maps are presented as a motivating and covered class for the log-Noetherian framework
A standard systematic class motivating the log-Noetherian structure and its applications, not used to disprove a claim.
Period integrals and period maps are standard geometric objects used as motivating definable examples in the log-Noetherian structure, not as failures of a claim.
Period integrals and period maps are a broad geometric family cited as a motivating application contained in the log-Noetherian/exponential structure.
Period integrals and period maps are a standard motivating class accommodated by the log-Noetherian/exp structure, not used adversarially.
period integrals and period maps are presented as a motivating application definable in the log-Noetherian/exponential structure
used as a motivating application class covered by the log-Noetherian structure, not as a failure case or structurally special object
Period integrals and period maps are presented as motivating and included examples for the log-Noetherian/definability framework, not as sporadic or boundary objects.
Presented as a motivating positive application of the log-Noetherian/exponential structure, with no sporadic or extremal structure.
presented as the motivating/application class captured by the log-Noetherian structure, not as a refutation
period integrals and period maps are presented as the motivating application included in the log-Noetherian structure
Period integrals and period maps are presented as motivating/applicative objects for the log-Noetherian structure, not as failures of a claim, and they form a systematic class.

examples.name: The unit sin(x)/x is log-Noetherian but not Noetherian
examples.note: After monomializing sin(x)=x·sin(x)/x, the unit sin(x)/x is shown not to be Noetherian by p-adic denominator growth, forcing the logarithmic category.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker factors sin x as x·sin(x)/x, analyzes derivatives at zero and p-adic denominators to argue the unit is log-Noetherian but not Noetherian

passes.reason: sin(x)/x is used to show that a log-Noetherian unit obtained by monomialization need not be Noetherian
Used to show that a log-Noetherian unit arising from monomializing sin(x) need not be Noetherian.
The unit sin(x)/x is used to show that the Noetherian class is not stable under extracting the monomial unit, motivating the genuinely broader log-Noetherian class.
The unit sinc function is used to refute the idea that monomializing a Noetherian function keeps the remaining unit Noetherian, motivating the log-Noetherian class.
The holomorphic unit sin(x)/x is a regular sinc-type function used to refute closure/sufficiency of ordinary Noetherian functions by being log-Noetherian but not Noetherian.
sin(x)/x is used to disprove the idea that the monomialized unit of a Noetherian function must remain Noetherian
used to prove the Noetherian class is insufficient since sin(x)/x is log-Noetherian but not Noetherian, though the function is not structurally sporadic or extremal
The function sin(x)/x is used to refute the idea that monomialization units or log-Noetherian functions must be Noetherian, while structurally it is a standard holomorphic unit.
Used to refute closure/sufficiency of ordinary Noetherian functions after monomialization, since sinc is log-Noetherian but not Noetherian.
used as a separating example showing a monomialization unit can be log-Noetherian but not Noetherian
the unit sin(x)/x is used to refute the expectation that the monomialized unit of a Noetherian function remains Noetherian

The unit sinc function sin(x)/x is exhibited to refute the idea that the Noetherian class suffices or is preserved under monomialization, while being an ordinary analytic unit rather than structurally exceptional.
